posts.name: David Rudd
posts.cooked: I am not familiar with this producer and had a 2019 Les Perrieres that I thought was quite good. Priced mid $50s. Have others had good experiences with them? Thanks

posts.name: J0e V@1eri
posts.cooked:
They’re ok. I always thought they had some connection with Jadot. The Kobrand guys were always trying to pedal them with Jadot tied in on presells.

We always did better with Guffens Heynen for PFs that were $50+. A lot better. Then again Ferret wines were much more approachable than Guffens but damn, GH Pouilly Fuisse was extremely special and had a following.
Jadot owns them. Jadot Pouilly Fuise is them. The regular J A Ferret Pouilly Fuise is nice and it was available for ~$30 not too long ago.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
Always enjoyed them, used to be a hell of a value closer to $30 for PF and $40-45 for Cru expressions, but still offer nice PF expressions at a fair price.

Not sure anything is Guffens Heynen.

@William_Kelley

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
I am a big fan. Went in a little extra on the 2020s. Like anything Jadot- very classic and often very restrained in youth. But lovely in time and always a bit understated. Not a wine I would put in your average tasting event- but with an uncomplicated meal they are often just the thing I want to open.

Nothing like Guffens-Heynen, which I admire very much but do not chase after personally.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: They’re run independently by the very serious Audrey Braccini and are one of the jewels in Jadot’s crown these days for me. The last handful of vintages have been among the best of the Mâconnais, achieving tension and cut even in sites that ripen early, in very ripe vintages. The top bottlings are quite age worthy and do tend to shut down, but patience pays dividends.

posts.name: Hank Victor
posts.cooked: I paid too much for the regular J A Ferret PF recently and was severely disappointed with the quality.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: What vintage and how did it disappoint?

posts.name: Hank Victor
posts.cooked:
William, it was the 2019 vintage and for full disclosure I paid $45.

I found the flavor concentration to be lacking and the fruit profile very restrained with mlf notes overshadowing the fruit. Structurally the acidity was racy and texture was nice but I expected more from the producer and price point. It could have been an off day for the wine and I even contemplated if it was my palate as my fiance thought the wine was very good. Palate was confirmed to be fine after drinking a bottle of Moric Blaufrankisch that showed exceptionally well with dinner.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Burgundy prices in the USA sure have escalated!

But I think the wine was likely just a bit shut down. The combination of DIAM closure plus correct levels of free sulfites (25 ppm or so) tends to have this effect, and I have generally found the wines to show best right after bottling, or 5+ years later.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: If the PF is $45 in your market, I’d look to try the Perrieres or Prouges for $60, or Hors Classe wines for $75.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: I’d be afraid to encounter mousiness in a Ferret wine.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked: Had the Prouges and Les Perrières in 17 and 18 and loved them. Some of the best white burgundy I’ve had for the money. I don’t recall having their base PF, but based on reading reviews, I do agree with @Kris_Patten that it seems there is a notable gap in quality between the Tête de Crus and the base.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked: Like others on this thread I was a big fan when the pricing was $30-35, less so at $50+, but I still think the wines are excellent
posts.display_username: Ben M a n d l e r
